questions.text: Which one of the following is the structure of Teflon?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Teflon is a synthetic polymer containing carbon and fluorine called polytetrafluoroethylene. Its formula is (C2F4)n.

The polymer represented by (-CF2-CCl2-)n is commonly known as poly(chlorotrifluoroethylene) or PCTFE. It is a fluoropolymer that exhibits excellent chemical resistance, high thermal stability, and low friction properties. PCTFE is used in various applications such as coatings, films, gaskets, and seals due to its exceptional resistance to chemicals and extreme temperatures.

The polymer represented by (-CH2-CH2-)n is commonly known as polyethylene, which is one of the most widely used polymers. Polyethylene has a range of applications due to its excellent physical and chemical properties, including its flexibility, low density, chemical resistance, and electrical insulation properties. It is used in various industries for packaging materials, plastic bags, containers, pipes, and many other everyday products.

The polymer represented by (-CF2-CH2-)n can be classified as a fluorinated polymer. The incorporation of fluorine atoms into the polymer chain can impart unique properties such as chemical resistance, low surface energy, and thermal stability. These polymers find applications in various fields, including coatings, lubricants, electrical insulation, and membranes.

questions.explanation: Teflon is a synthetic polymer containing carbon and fluorine called polytetrafluoroethylene. Its formula is (C2F4)n.

questions.text: The ―NH―CO is called:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While the ―NH―CO bond is indeed characteristic of an amide group, this term doesn't specifically describe the entire structure or its role.

This option is incorrect. The amino group (―NH₂) consists of a lone pair of electrons on a nitrogen atom. It's not the same as the amide bond (―NH―CO), which involves both nitrogen and carbonyl (carbon-oxygen) groups.
This option is incorrect. This term doesn't accurately define the ―NH―CO bond. Proteins are made up of amino acids connected by peptide linkages (―NH―CO―), not just the bond itself.
-NH-CO- is a peptide linkage bond, done when 2 amino acids are joined together. Thus, this option is correct.

questions.explanation: -NH-CO- is a peptide linkage bond, done when 2 amino acids are joined together.

questions.text: At intermediate value of pH, amino acids form Zwitter ions containing:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Intermediate value means under neutral conditions. So in that circumstance amino acids produce NH3+ and COO- zwitter ions.
This option is incorrect because at intermediate pH, the amino group (-NH2) tends to be protonated to form -NH3+ (positively charged), not -NH3 (neutral).
This option is incorrect because at intermediate pH, the carboxyl group (-COOH) tends to lose a proton and become -COO- (negatively charged), not remain as -COOH.
This option is incorrect because both the amino group (-NH2) and the carboxyl group (-COOH) are expected to undergo protonation and deprotonation, resulting in the formation of -NH3+ and -COO- ions.

questions.explanation: Intermediate value means under neutral conditions. So in that circumstance amino acids produce NH3+ and COO- zwitter ions.

questions.text: If the primer annealing temperature is increased to 94°C . What will happen ?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: If the primer annealing temperature is increased to 94°C, it is unlikely that annealing will occur, as this temperature is above the melting temperature (Tm) of most primer-template duplexes.Annealing is the process of the primers binding to the complementary sequences on the template DNA during a PCR cycle. It typically occurs at a temperature that is 5°C to 10°C below the melting temperature (Tm) of the primer-template duplex. Hence, it is an incorrect option.
This word is not related to the question statement. So, this option is incorrect.

Primer annealing is a critical step in polymerase chain reaction or PCR. In this step, the primers bind to flanking sequences of the target DNA for amplification. If the primer annealing temperature is increased to 94°C non annealing occurs. At high temperature Non annealing occurs and the whole process is disturbed.

If the annealing temperature is increased to 94°C, this is above the Tm of most primer-template duplexes, and the primers will not be able to bind to the template DNA. Instead, other processes may occur, such as non-specific binding or primer-dimer formation. Hence, it is an incorrect option.

questions.explanation: Primer annealing is a critical step in polymerase chain reaction or PCR. In this step, the primers bind to flanking sequences of the target DNA for amplification. If the primer annealing temperature is increased to 94°C non annealing occurs. At high temperature Non annealing occurs and the whole process is disturbed.

questions.text: Common name of Octadecanoic acid is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Propionic acid has the IUPAC name propanoic acid, which is a short-chain fatty acid, not related to octadecanoic acid.
Stearic acid is the common name for octadecanoic acid, which is a long-chain saturated fatty acid with 18 carbon atoms.
Palmitic acid corresponds to hexadecanoic acid, with a 16-carbon chain, differing from octadecanoic acid.
Formic acid, known as methanoic acid, is the simplest carboxylic acid and is not related to octadecanoic acid.
Acetic acid, or ethanoic acid, is a common component of vinegar and is not the same as octadecanoic acid.

questions.explanation: Stearic acid (IUPAC systematic name: octadecanoic acid is one of the useful types of saturated fatty acids that comes from many animal and vegetable fats and oils.

questions.text: All are dehydrating agents for alcohols to produce alkenes except:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: By passing the vapors of an alcohol over alumina (Al2O3) at 623 K (350°C), it indicates a dehydration reaction is taking place.
There is no such reaction in which P2O5 can produce alkenes from alcohols.

Sulfuric acid is a strong dehydrating agent that can remove water molecules from alcohols through the elimination of a water molecule (H2O). It is widely used in various dehydration reactions due to its strong acidic nature and ability to facilitate the removal of water.
Phosphoric acid can act as both a catalyst and a dehydrating agent. It can facilitate the removal of water from alcohols, leading to the formation of alkenes through an elimination reaction.

questions.explanation: There is no such reaction in which P2O5 can produce alkenes from alcohol. Dehydration of alcohols can happen through Al2O3, Dil H2SO4, and H3PO4, more about these reactions:

In the z=0 configuration below, the centre chloride ion belongs to two unit cells in the overall crystal.

questions.explanation: In the z=0 configuration below, the center chloride ion actually belongs to two unit cells in the overall crystal.

questions.text: Graphite has a structural similarity with:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.B2H6 (Diborane): This has a unique "banana" structure and does not have a similarity to graphite.
This option is incorrect. B4C (Boron Carbide): This has a more complex structure and does not resemble the planar hexagonal arrangement of graphite.
This option is incorrect. B (Boron): Boron alone does not form a structure similar to graphite.

H-BN or BN structure consists of an alternate arrangement of Boron and Nitrogen atoms in a 2-dimensional plane and turn, forms a hexagonal ring structure.The N and B atoms are combined by an sp, orbital forming a strong sigma bond. Due to the formation of hexagonal rings, they share a common structure but with different atoms.

questions.explanation: H−BN or BN structure consists of an alternate arrangement of Boron and Nitrogen atoms in a 2-dimensional plane and turn, forms a hexagonal ring structure. The N and B atoms are combined by an sp2 orbital forming a strong sigma bond. Due to the formation of hexagonal rings, they share a common structure but with different atoms.

questions.text: Which is the structure of polyvinyl chloride (polychloroethane)?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is incorrect. This structure would represent polytetrachloroethylene rather than polyvinyl chloride (PVC).
This is incorrect. This structure suggests that both carbon atoms have a chlorine substituent, which is not the case for PVC.
This is incorrect. This represents vinyl chloride (chloroethene), which is the monomer used to form polyvinyl chloride (PVC) but is not the polymer structure itself.

Poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC) is a synthetic resin made from vinyl chloride and is a member of a large family of polymers broadly referred to as “vinyls.” The chemical formula for vinyl chloride is H2C=CHCl, and the formula for PVC is (H2C–CHCl)n, where n is the degree of polymerization.

questions.explanation: Poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC) is a synthetic resin made from vinyl chloride and is a member of a large family of polymers broadly referred to as “vinyls.” The chemical formula for vinyl chloride is H2C=CHCl, and the formula for PVC is (H2C–CHCl)n, where n is the degree of polymerization.

questions.text: _ is used as a preservative for life specimens.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: H2SO4 (sulfuric acid) is a strong acid and a dehydrating agent. It is not suitable for preserving life specimens as it can cause severe tissue damage.
Ammonia is a toxic chemical that can be corrosive to tissues, making it unsuitable for preserving biological specimens.
Methanol is generally not recommended for preserving specimens for molecular studies, as it can negatively affect DNA preservation, particularly in insects.
NaOH (sodium hydroxide) is a strong base used for cleaning and disinfection, but it is not used for preserving biological specimens due to its protein and nucleic acid degrading properties.

Formalin, a solution of formaldehyde in water, is commonly used for preserving biological specimens. It is effective because it hardens tissues and prevents decay by acting as a disinfectant and antiseptic.

questions.explanation: Formalin is widely used for the preservation of biological specimens due to its ability to fix tissues, making them resistant to decay. It works by crosslinking proteins, which helps maintain the structure of cells and tissues. Formalin also has antiseptic properties, preventing microbial growth. The other options, such as H2SO4, ammonia, methanol, and NaOH, are not suitable for preservation as they can damage tissues or fail to effectively preserve cellular structures.

questions.text: All are dehydrating agents for alcohols to produce alkene except:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Alumina (Al2O3) acts as a catalyst and a dehydrating agent when alcohol vapors are passed over it at high temperatures, facilitating the formation of alkenes.
P2O5 (phosphorus pentoxide) is known for its strong dehydrating properties, making it effective in converting alcohols to alkenes.
Dilute H2SO4 typically facilitates hydration reactions rather than dehydration. Its concentrated form is required for dehydration processes.
H3PO4 (phosphoric acid) can act as a dehydrating agent, promoting the conversion of alcohols to alkenes through elimination reactions.

questions.explanation: Dilute H2SO4, on the other hand, generally does not promote dehydration; instead, concentrated H2SO4 is the form that acts as a strong dehydrating agent.

options.explanation: Propionic acid has the IUPAC name propanoic acid, which is a short-chain fatty acid, not related to octadecanoic acid.
Stearic acid is the common name for octadecanoic acid, which is a long-chain saturated fatty acid with 18 carbon atoms.

questions.explanation: Stearic acid (IUPAC systematic name: octadecanoic acid is one of the useful types of saturated fatty acids that comes from many animal and vegetable fats and oils.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a “raw material” used in the Solvay process?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Sodium chloride (NaCl) is a key raw material in the Solvay process, used to provide chloride ions.
Sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) is the end product of the Solvay process and is not a raw material.
Limestone (calcium carbonate, CaCO3) is a raw material in the Solvay process, providing carbonate ions.
Ammonia gas (NH3) is used in the Solvay process to react with brine and form ammonium ions.

questions.explanation: Sodium carbonate is the product of the Solvay process so it can definitely not be the raw material required for this process.

questions.text: Which of the following componds is insoluble in water?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Calcium stearate is a fatty acid salt that forms a precipitate in water, making it insoluble. Its hydrophobic nature prevents it from interacting with water molecules, leading to its characteristic as a soap scum, particularly in hard water conditions.
This compound is a sulfonic acid salt that typically exhibits better solubility in water due to the presence of the sulfonate group, which can interact favorably with water.
Similar to the previous option, this compound has a sulfonate group that enhances its solubility in water, making it soluble rather than insoluble.
Potassium palmitate is a salt of a fatty acid that tends to be more soluble in water due to the presence of the potassium ion, which interacts favorably with water molecules.

questions.explanation: The calcium salt of stearic acid is the primary component of soap scum. It is formed when soap reacts with calcium ions in hard water and is notoriously insoluble, which is why soap is ineffective in hard water. Salts of sulfonic acids (found in detergents) are generally soluble.

questions.text: Which one of the following is addition polymer:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Nylon is a type of condensation polymer called a polyamide.
Polyvinyl chloride is produced in an addition polymerisation reaction using the chloroethene (vinyl chloride) monomer.
Polyethylene is an addition polymer that is created by the polymerization of ethylene monomer units.

In addition polymerization (sometimes called chain-growth polymerization), a chain reaction adds new monomer units to the growing polymer molecule one at a time through double or triple bonds in the monomerOn the other hand, condensation polymerization is a process that involves repeated condensation reactions between tri-functional or bi-functional monomers with elimination of small molecules.

questions.explanation: In addition polymerization (sometimes called chain-growth polymerization), a chain reaction adds new monomer units to the growing polymer molecule one at a time through double or triple bonds in the monomerOn the other hand, condensation polymerization is a process that involves repeated condensation reactions between tri-functional or bi-functional monomers with elimination of small molecules.

questions.text: Bakelite is a polymer of formaldehyde and _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Bakelite is synthesized through the polymerization of phenol and formaldehyde. This process involves a condensation reaction that produces a durable and heat-resistant material.
While ethanol can react with formaldehyde, it does not form Bakelite. Instead, it forms hemiacetals and acetals, which are not used in the production of this specific polymer.
Butanol typically does not react with formaldehyde to form a polymer like Bakelite. Its reactivity is limited in this context, making it an incorrect choice.
The reaction of methanol with formaldehyde leads to the formation of methylene glycol but does not contribute to the creation of Bakelite. Therefore, this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: Bakelite is the first synthetic thermosetting polymer. It is formed by the condensation polymerization of phenol and formaldehyde. The resulting polymer is hard, heat-resistant, and electrically non-conductive, making it useful in electrical and household products.

questions.text: The polymer containing acrylonitrile as a monomer is:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.explanation: Acrylonitrile monomers join to form polyacrylonitrile (PAN), which is also called polyvinyl cyanide or Creslan 61. It has the formula (C3H3N)n. It is a synthetic resin and is used in the manufacture of carbon fibres, clothing and furnishings.

questions.text: In how many types polymers are classified?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: While there are many polymers in existence, they are not typically classified into five main types. This option overcomplicates the classification.
Although there are various categories of polymers, they are commonly grouped into three types based on their sources, making this option incorrect.
This option is correct as polymers are classified primarily into natural and synthetic categories, with a third category of semi-synthetic sometimes mentioned.

This option may appear valid since there are three categories of polymers when considering natural, synthetic, and semi-synthetic; however, the question specifically asks how many types they are classified into, which is generally recognized as two main categories.

questions.explanation: Polymers are classified based on their nature into two main types: natural and synthetic. Natural polymers occur in nature, e.g., cellulose, starch, and proteins. Synthetic polymers are man-made, e.g., nylon, polyester, and polyethylene.

questions.text: Polymerization is of how many types:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.explanation: Polymerization reactions are of two types; condensation and addition polymerization. Condensation polymerization involves combination of two monomers with loss of a group, such as water or ammonia. Addition polymerization involves breaking of double bonds as two monomers combine, eg. the polymerization of ethene to form polyethene.

options.explanation: Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) is an addition polymer of chloroethene and has a general formula of (C2H3Cl)n.
Incorrect. PVC is formed through addition polymerization, where monomers join without the elimination of any by-products.
Incorrect. PVC is a synthetic polymer derived from petroleum, not a naturally occurring biopolymer.
Incorrect. PVC is a thermoplastic polymer, which can be melted and reshaped upon heating, unlike thermosetting polymers that become permanently rigid upon curing.

questions.text: Which one of the following compounds is an additional polymer?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: An 'addition polymer' is a polymer which consists of monomers, that add on to each other or combine without the formation of a secondary product, whereas in 'condensation polymers', the functional groups of monomers condense, such that a secondary product, such as H2O, is also formed. Out of all these options, only option A is an addition polymer.
Out of all these options, only option A is an addition polymer, whereas all three others are condensation polymers.
Out of all these options, only option A is an addition polymer, whereas all three others are condensation polymers.
Out of all these options, only option A is an addition polymer, whereas all three others are condensation polymers.

Out of all these options, only option A is an addition polymer, whereas all three others are condensation polymers.

questions.text: Macromolecules are described as large molecules built up from small repeating units known as:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A macromolecule is a very large molecule. That are composed of many covalently bonded atoms. Macromolecules are polymers of smaller molecules called monomers.
Isomers are molecules with the same molecular formula but different structural arrangements. They don't involve repeating units, so this option is incorrect.
Metameres aren't a recognized term in the context of macromolecules or chemistry. This option is incorrect due to its lack of relevance.

Tautomers are isomers that exist in dynamic equilibrium due to the movement of a hydrogen atom and a double bond. They aren't associated with the concept of macromolecules and monomers. This option is also incorrect.

questions.explanation: A macromolecule is a very large molecule. That are composed of many covalently bonded atoms. Macromolecules are polymers of smaller molecules called monomers.

questions.text: Terylene, a polyester is an example of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Terylene is not a biopolymer as it is a synthetic polymer derived from petrochemicals, not from natural sources like plants or animals.
Terylene is not a lipid. Lipids are a diverse group of organic molecules including fats, oils, and phospholipids, while Terylene is a type of polyester polymer.
PolyESTERS (polymers of esters) are mostly made by condensation polymerisation reactions, making them condensation polymers.

Terylene is not an addition polymer. Addition polymers are formed through the repeated addition of monomer units without the elimination of any byproducts, whereas Terylene is a condensation polymer formed by the elimination of water molecules during polymerization.

questions.explanation: PolyESTERS (polymers of esters) are mostly made by condensation polymerisation reactions, making them condensation polymers.

questions.text: Polymers are made up of:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.explanation: Polymers are large molecules made up of small, repeated units called monomers. Examples include polyvinyl chloride, polyester, cellulose, and glycogen.

questions.text: What is the chemical reaction mechanism by which cells make polymers from monomers?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. A dehydration reaction, also known as a condensation reaction, involves the removal of a water molecule to form a covalent bond between two monomers, resulting in the formation of a polymer.

This option refers to a specific type of bond that can form between cysteine residues in proteins, but it is not the general mechanism for polymer formation from monomers. Disulphide bridges are important for the stabilization of protein structure, not for polymerization.

Ionic bonding can occur between charged molecules, but it is not the primary mechanism for the formation of polymers from monomers. Polymers are typically formed through covalent bonds, not ionic bonds.
Hydrolysis is the process of breaking down polymers into monomers by adding water, which is the opposite of the polymerization process. Therefore, it is not the correct answer.

questions.explanation: Cells form polymers (like proteins, polysaccharides, and nucleic acids) from monomers via dehydration reactions. in this process, a water molecule is removed a new band forms between monomers. This is the primary mechanism. for building macromolecules in biological systems.

questions.text: Polymers are made up of:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.explanation: Polymers are large molecules made up of small, repeated units called monomers. Examples include polyvinyl chloride, polyester, cellulose, and glycogen.

questions.text: _ is a macromolecule found in blood.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Hemoglobin is a protein in red blood cells that helps blood carry oxygen throughout the body.(Hemoglobin contains iron, which gives blood its red color.) The hemoglobin count is an indirect measurement of the number of red blood cells in your body.
Plasma, is not a macromolecule but rather the liquid component of blood that contains various dissolved substances, including electrolytes, proteins, and hormones.
Creatinine is a small molecule produced by the breakdown of creatine phosphate in muscle tissue and is excreted by the kidneys. It is not a macromolecule found in the blood.
Plasmids are small, circular DNA molecules found in some bacteria that can replicate independently of the bacterial chromosome. They are not found in the blood.

questions.explanation: Option A: Hemoglobin is a protein in red blood cells that helps blood carry oxygen throughout the body. (Hemoglobin contains iron, which gives blood its red colour.) The haemoglobin count is an indirect measurement of the number of red blood cells in your body.Option B: Plasma, is not a macromolecule but rather the liquid component of blood that contains various dissolved substances, including electrolytes, proteins, and hormones.Option C: Creatinine, is a small molecule produced by the breakdown of creatine phosphate in muscle tissue and is excreted by the kidneys. It is not a macromolecule found in the blood.Option D: Plasmids are small, circular DNA molecules found in some bacteria that can replicate independently of the bacterial chromosome. They are not found in the blood.

questions.text: Non-protein part of conjugated protein is called _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A: Coenzymes are small organic molecules that assist enzymes in carrying out specific reactions. They often act as carriers of chemical groups or electrons during enzymatic reactions. While coenzymes are important for enzyme function, they are not considered the non-protein part of a conjugated protein.

A Co-factor is a non-protein molecule or ion that is required for the proper functioning of an enzyme. Co-factors can be either inorganic ions, such as metal ions, or organic molecules. They help enzymes catalyze reactions by providing additional chemical groups or facilitating certain steps of the reaction. However, co-factors are not typically part of conjugated proteins.

The prosthetic group refers to the non-protein component of a conjugated protein. It is a tightly bound molecule that is essential for the protein’s structure and function. Prosthetic groups can be various types of molecules, such as metal ions or organic molecules like heme in hemoglobin. They play a crucial role in the protein’s overall function and often participate directly in the protein’s catalytic activity or ligand binding.
This option indicates that none of the provided options is the correct answer. However, in this case, the correct answer is indeed option C, prosthetic group.

questions.text: Amino acids in proteins are held together by:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.explanation: Amino acids are held by peptide bonds.

questions.text: In nature fatty acids occur as _ of glycerol?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Fatty acids occur as esters of alcohols, rather than as alcohols themselves. Specifically, fatty acids are typically found as esters of glycerol in triglycerides, which are the main constituents of natural fats and oils

questions.explanation: Fatty acids occur as esters of alcohols, rather than as alcohols themselves. Specifically, fatty acids are typically found as esters of glycerol in triglycerides, which are the main constituents of natural fats and oils

questions.text: Proteoses enzyme & peptones belong to which type of protein?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Simple proteins are composed of only amino acid residues linked together by peptide bonds. Examples of simple proteins include albumins and globulins

Proteoses, enzymes, and peptones are all types of derived proteins. Derived proteins are formed by the partial hydrolysis of a protein molecule. Proteins can be hydrolyzed by either acids or enzymes to yield smaller peptides and amino acids.

Conjugated proteins, on the other hand, are proteins that contain non-protein components, such as lipids, carbohydrates, or metals, in addition to amino acid residues. Examples of conjugated proteins include hemoglobin, which contains an iron-containing heme group, and glycoproteins, which contain carbohydrate groups.
This option is incorrect

Examples of conjugated proteins include hemoglobin, which contains an iron-containing heme group, and glycoproteins, which contain carbohydrate groups. This option is incorrect

questions.text: Enzymes that catalyze the addition of ammonia, water, or carbon dioxide to double bond or their removal are called _ ?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Lyases, also known as lyases, are a class of enzymes that catalyze the breaking of various chemical bonds in organic molecules, without the addition of water. They typically act on C-C, C-O, C-N, and other bonds, and are involved in various metabolic pathways, including the synthesis and degradation of carbohydrates, amino acids, and nucleotides.
Hydrolases are a class of enzymes that catalyze the hydrolysis of various chemical bonds in organic molecules, using water as a co-substrate.
Ligases are a class of enzymes that catalyze the joining of two molecules by forming a new chemical bond between them, often utilizing ATP as a source of energy
Transferases are a class of enzymes that catalyze the transfer of a chemical group from one molecule to another molecule

questions.explanation: Lyases, also known as lyases, are a class of enzymes that catalyze the breaking of various chemical bonds in organic molecules, without the addition of water. They typically act on C-C, C-O, C-N, and other bonds, and are involved in various metabolic pathways, including the synthesis and degradation of carbohydrates, amino acids, and nucleotides. Hydrolases are a class of enzymes that catalyze the hydrolysis of various chemical bonds in organic molecules, using water as a co-substrate. Ligases are a class of enzymes that catalyze the joining of two molecules by forming a new chemical bond between them, often utilizing ATP as a source of energy. Transferases are a class of enzymes that catalyze the transfer of a chemical group from one molecule to another molecule.

questions.text: Which of the following element is not usually present in all proteins?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Carbon is a fundamental element found in all organic compounds, including proteins. It forms the backbone of amino acids, which are the building blocks of proteins. Carbon atoms in amino acids are bonded to hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and sometimes sulfur atoms, forming the diverse structures found in proteins.

Hydrogen is another essential element present in all proteins. It is found in the amino and carboxyl groups of amino acids, as well as in the side chains of certain amino acids. Hydrogen bonds also play crucial roles in stabilizing the secondary and tertiary structures of proteins.

Nitrogen is a key component of proteins, mainly because it is found in the amino groups (-NH2) of amino acids. Nitrogen atoms participate in peptide bond formation, which links amino acids together to form polypeptide chains. Additionally, nitrogen is present in the side chains of some amino acids, contributing to the diversity of protein structures and functions.

The element that is not usually present in all proteins is Sulfur. While carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen are found in all proteins, sulfur is present only in certain amino acids, such as cysteine and methionine, which contain sulfur atoms in their side chains. Therefore, sulfur is not universally present in all proteins.

questions.explanation: All proteins contain carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen. Carbon forms the backbone of amino acids, while hydrogen is found in amino and carboxyl groups. Nitrogen is essential for peptide bond formation and is present in the amino groups of amino acids. However, sulfur is not universally present in all proteins; it is found only in certain amino acids like cysteine and methionine. Therefore, sulfur is the element that is not usually present in all proteins.

The critical temperature of a substance is the temperature at and above which vapor of the substance cannot be liquefied, no matter how much pressure is applied.
The temperature at which enzyme activity is maximum is called the optimum temperature. Optimum temperatures have conditions in which a particular enzyme is most active.

questions.text: Enzymes are also called as _ ?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is wrong because enzymes are not only called catalysts. They are biocatalysts.
This option is wrong because enzymes are not lipoproteins. They are proteins.
This option is wrong because enzymes are not conjugated molecules.

Enzymes are proteins that catalyze a chemical reaction in our body. They function as a catalyst that speeds up the reaction by lowering the activation energy. The enzyme accelerates a chemical reaction without changing its equilibrium, so it is called as a biocatalyst.

questions.text: Dehydrogenase is an example of which class of enzyme?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A dehydrogenase is an enzyme belonging to the group of oxidoreductases that oxidizes a substrate by reducing an electron acceptor, usually NAD+/NADP+ or a flavin coenzyme such as FAD or FMN.
Hydrolases are the group of enzymes that catalyze bond cleavages by reaction with water.
In biochemistry, a ligase is an enzyme that can catalyze the joining of two molecules by forming a new chemical bond.
Transferase is any one of a class of enzymes that catalyse the transfer of specific functional groups from one molecule to another.

questions.explanation: Option: A A dehydrogenase is an enzyme belonging to the group of oxidoreductases that oxidizes a substrate by reducing an electron acceptor, usually NAD+/NADP+ or a flavin coenzyme such as FAD or FMN. Option B: Hydrolases are a group of enzymes that catalyze bond cleavages by reaction with water. Option C: In biochemistry, a ligase is an enzyme that can catalyze the joining of two molecules by forming a new chemical bond. Option D: Transferase is any one of a class of enzymes that catalyse the transfer of specific functional groups from one molecule to another.

questions.text: The protein component of an enzyme is called _?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: coenzyme is defined as an organic molecule that binds to the active sites of certain enzymes to assist in the catalysis of a reaction. More specifically, coenzymes can function as intermediate carriers of electrons during these reactions or be transferred between enzymes as functional groups.

Cofactors are key components of enzyme pathways that facilitate the activity or regulation of enzymes. Cofactors can be metals (e.g. magnesium, copper) or organic compounds (e.g. heme, sugars, proteins).
Apoenzyme or apoprotein is an enzymatically inactive protein part of an enzyme, which requires a cofactor for its activity.
Prosthetic group is a type of a helper molecule which is a nonproteinaceous compound that helps enzymes to perform their functions.

questions.explanation: Apoenzyme or apoprotein is an enzymatically inactive protein part of an enzyme, which requires a cofactor for its activity.

questions.text: The catalytic activity of an enzyme is enhanced by?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Enzyme activators are chemical compounds that increase a velocity of enzymatic reaction.

Cofactors are key components of enzyme pathways that facilitate the activity or regulation of enzymes. Cofactors can be metals (e.g. magnesium, copper) or organic compounds (e.g. heme, sugars, proteins).

questions.explanation: Following are definitions of each of the above terms: Coenzyme: A substance that enhances the action of an enzyme. (An enzyme is a protein that functions as a catalyst to mediate and speed a chemical reaction). Coenzymes are small molecules. They cannot by themselves catalyze a reaction but they can help enzymes to do soCofactors: can be metals or small organic molecules, and their primary function is to assist (not enhance) in enzyme activity. They are able to assist in performing certain, necessary, reactions the enzyme cannot perform alone. Activators: Enzyme activators are chemical compounds that increase a velocity (enhances) of enzymatic reaction

questions.text: Which of the following element is not present in all proteins ?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A is correct since sulphur is present only in some proteins, not all. The general formula for proteins is RCH(NH2)COOH implying that ALL the proteins at least contain C, H, N and O
(C) Carbon – Incorrect: Carbon is a fundamental element in all organic molecules, including proteins. It forms the backbone of amino acids and peptide bonds.
(N) Nitrogen – Incorrect: All proteins contain nitrogen because amino acids, the building blocks of proteins, have an amino group (-NH₂) that contains nitrogen.
(O) Oxygen – Incorrect: Oxygen is present in all proteins as part of the carboxyl (-COOH) and amide (-CONH₂) groups in amino acids.

questions.text: The most abundant protein in animals, forming 25%–35% of body protein, is which type of protein?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Simple proteins are those that are made up of amino acid units only, joined by peptide bonds. Upon hydrolysis, that yields a mixture of amino acids and nothing else. Simple proteins: This is the correct answer. Simple proteins are proteins that consist only of amino acids without any non-protein components. They are the most common and abundant type of protein found in living organisms. The most abundant protein in animals, forming 25–35% of body protein, belongs to the category of simple proteins.

Conjugated protein: This option is incorrect. Conjugated proteins are proteins that contain a non-protein component in addition to amino acids. Examples of conjugated proteins include glycoproteins (protein + carbohydrate), lipoproteins (protein + lipid), and nucleoproteins(protein and nucleic acid). The most abundant protein in animals, which forms 25–35% of bodyprotein is not a conjugated protein.

Derived protein: This option is incorrect. Derived proteins are formed from the breakdown or modification of other proteins. They are not primary structural proteins but are instead derived from existing proteins through processes such as enzymatic cleavage or post-translational modifications. The most abundant protein in animals, forming 25–35% of body protein, is not a derived protein.

options.text: None of these options are correct
options.explanation: None of the above: This option is incorrect. The correct answer is not "none of the above." The most abundant protein in animals, which makes up 25–35% of body protein, belongs to a specific category of proteins.

questions.explanation: 'Simple proteins' are those that are made of amino acid units only, joined by peptide bonds. Upon hydrolysis, they yield a mixture of amino acids and nothing else. 'Conjugated proteins' are composed of simple proteins combined with a non-proteinous substance. The non-proteinous substance is called the 'prosthetic' group or cofactor. 'Derived proteins' are not naturally occurring proteins and are obtained from simple proteins by the action of enzymes and chemical agents. Since both conjugated and derived types arise from simple proteins, the amount of simple proteins in the body has to be the greatest. Moreover, the functionality of simple proteins is so vast that the quantity required for them is large.

questions.text: The activator of the carbonic anhydrase enzyme is _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Mn2+ is incorrect as an activator of the carbonic anhydrase enzyme. While manganese(Mn) is indeed a cofactor for some enzymes, it is not known to directly activate carbonicanhydrase. Carbonic anhydrase is a zinc metalloenzyme, meaning it requires the presence ofzinc (Zn2+) as an essential cofactor for its enzymatic activity.

Mg2+ is incorrect as well. Magnesium (Mg) is not an activator of carbonic anhydrase.While magnesium ions play important roles in various enzymatic reactions in the body, includingAs cofactors, they are not directly involved in activating carbonic anhydrase.
Zn+2 is the activator for carbonic anhydrase.

None of these is also incorrect.As mentioned earlier, the correct activator of carbonicanhydrase is zinc (Zn2+). The binding of zinc ions to the active site of carbonic anhydrasecrucial for the enzyme's catalytic activity, facilitating the conversion of carbon dioxide and waterinto carbonic acid.

questions.explanation: Zn2+ is the activator of carbonic anhydrase.

questions.text: The temperature at which enzyme activity is maximum is called as_?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The temperature at which enzyme activity is maximum is called the "optimum temperature." Enzymes are sensitive to changes in temperature, and each enzyme has an optimal temperature range at which it functions most efficiently. This optimal temperature is the temperature at which the enzyme catalyzes the reaction at its maximum rate.

Absolute temperature refers to temperature measured on the Kelvin scale (K). The Kelvin scale is an absolute temperature scale that starts from absolute zero, the lowest possible temperature at which particles have minimal motion and all thermal motion ceases.

The critical temperature is a property of a substance, especially relevant in the study of phase transitions and critical phenomena in physics and thermodynamics. It is the temperature above which a substance cannot exist in the liquid state, regardless of the pressure applied. At the critical temperature, the distinction between the liquid and gaseous phases disappears, and the substance is said to be in a critical state.

The temperature at which enzyme activity is maximum is called the "optimum temperature." Enzymes are sensitive to changes in temperature, and each enzyme has an optimal temperature range at which it functions most efficiently. This optimal temperature is the temperature at which the enzyme catalyzes the reaction at its maximum rate.

questions.explanation: The temperature at which enzyme activity is maximum is called the "optimum temperature." Enzymes are sensitive to changes in temperature, and each enzyme has an optimal temperature range at which it functions most efficiently. This optimal temperature is the temperature at which the enzyme catalyzes the reaction at its maximum rate.

questions.text: The enzyme which is used in treatment of cancer in children?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Thrombin is not an enzyme that is commonly used for the treatment of cancer in children. Thrombin is a protein that is involved in the blood clotting process.

There are several types of enzymes that are currently being studied for the treatment of cancer in children. One example is L-asparaginase, which is used to treat acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) in children. L-asparaginase works by breaking down the amino acid asparagine in the blood, which is necessary for the growth and survival of cancer cells. By depriving cancer cells of asparagine, L-asparaginase can help to slow or stop the growth of cancer cells.
